title: Using pseudorandom number generators (PRNGs) is security-sensitive
description: Using pseudorandom number generators (PRNGs) is security-sensitiveAnalyze your coderesponsibility-trustworthysecuritySecurity HotspotcwePRNGs are algorithms that produce sequences of numbers that only approximate true randomness. While they are suitable for applications like simulations or modeling, they are not appropriate for security-sensitive contexts because their outputs can be predictable if the internal state is known.In contrast, cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generators (CSPRNGs) are designed to be secure against prediction attacks. CSPRNGs use cryptographic algorithms to ensure that the generated sequences are not only random but also unpredictable, even if part of the sequence or the internal state becomes known.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
var random = new Random();
var token = random.Next().ToString();

byte[] key = new byte[32];
new Random().NextBytes(key);

checks.message: Using System.Random is security-sensitive. For cryptographic purposes, use RandomNumberGenerator instead

logic.detection_conditions: Creation of System.Random instance without explicit seed
logic.triggering_conditions: new Random() constructor call (unseeded - potentially used for security)
never_trigger_on: new Random(seed) - explicitly seeded for reproducibility (likely non-security use)
logic.reasoning: System.Random is a pseudorandom number generator that is NOT cryptographically secure. Its output can be predicted if the seed is known or guessed. For security-sensitive operations like generating tokens, session IDs, or cryptographic keys, RandomNumberGenerator (CSPRNG) should be used instead. Seeded Random instances are excluded as they indicate intentional non-security use (reproducibility).
